coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive methodology for characterization and electro-thermal modelling for a next-generation solid-state battery

Micó, Carlos
Cobo, Mariany Chávez

Vemula, Jagadish Babu
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state batteries (SSBs) offer higher energy densities, improved safety from non-flammable solid electrolytes, and longer lifespans compared to traditional lithium-ion batteries. However, modeling SSBs presents unique challenges due to their distinct electrical and thermal behaviors. This study presents an experimental characterization and electro-thermal model for a next-generation 30 Ah polymer-based SSBs. This work integrates experimental characterization, electrical modeling using a third-order equivalent circuit, and thermal modeling with a lumped thermal mass to represent temperature dynamics. Through comprehensive testing, including open-circuit voltage and hybrid pulse power characterization, we identified dependencies of key parameters on state of charge and temperature. Two-way coupling is used between the electrical and thermal models, where the first feeds the second one with the heat generated while the thermal model provides the temperature to update electrical parameters accordingly. This approach accounts for both irreversible heat from internal resistance and reversible heat from entropy changes, determined through a validated method correlating open-circuit voltage and temperature. Results demonstrate that the proposed model can simulate the dynamic interactions between the electric and thermal behavior of a SSB and predicts the electric performance as well as the cell surface temperature with high accuracy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state batteries (SSBs) offer higher energy densities, improved safety from non-flammable solid electrolytes, and longer lifespans compared to traditional lithium-ion batteries. However, modeling SSBs presents unique challenges due to their distinct electrical and thermal behaviors. This study presents an experimental characterization and electro-thermal model for a next-generation 30 Ah polymer-based SSBs. This work integrates experimental characterization, electrical modeling using a third-order equivalent circuit, and thermal modeling with a lumped thermal mass to represent temperature dynamics. Through comprehensive testing, including open-circuit voltage and hybrid pulse power characterization, we identified dependencies of key parameters on state of charge and temperature. Two-way coupling is used between the electrical and thermal models, where the first feeds the second one with the heat generated while the thermal model provides the temperature to update electrical parameters accordingly. This approach accounts for both irreversible heat from internal resistance and reversible heat from entropy changes, determined through a validated method correlating open-circuit voltage and temperature. Results demonstrate that the proposed model can simulate the dynamic interactions between the electric and thermal behavior of a SSB and predicts the electric performance as well as the cell surface temperature with high accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become the cornerstone of energy storage for electric vehicles (EVs) due to their high energy and power densities, exceptional cyclability, and good reliability. The recent advancement for vehicle electrification was only possible with the cost-effective mass production of LIBs alongside their continuous performance enhancements [1]. However, LIBs face inherent limitations that impede further advancements, prompting intensive research into alternative cell technologies. Among these, solid-state batteries (SSBs) stand out as a promising successor, offering potentially higher energy and power densities due to the possibility of bipolar stacking, the potential usage of the lithium metal or silicon anode, and projected higher device safety. Moreover, SSBs are anticipated to enhance device safety significantly as they replace flammable liquid electrolytes with solid ones, thereby mitigating one of the primary causes of LIBs' long-term instability. Additionally, the high lithium-ion transference number of inorganic solid electrolytes, which approaches unity, enables high-speed charging capabilities without electrolyte polarization, leading to high projected power densities [2].

An SSBs operates similarly to traditional liquid electrolyte batteries, with a key distinction being the use of a solid electrolyte. The fundamental components of a SSBs include the anode, cathode, and the electrolyte, each playing a crucial role in the battery's function [3,4]. The anode, typically bonded to copper foil, enhances the battery's electrical conductivity [5]. During the charge cycle, lithium ions from the cathode structure migrate towards the solid electrolyte interface. These ions then traverse through the solid electrolyte to intercalate into the carbon layers of the anode. Conversely, during discharge, lithium ions move back through the electrolyte to re-enter the cathode particles, completing the cycle [6,7]. Fig. 1 is a schematic diagram of a lithium based solid state battery, the curved arrows indicate the movement of the Li-ions during the charging and the discharging process, respectively. The electrons produced due to the reaction are used to drive a load in the external circuit.

While solid-state batteries enable high-specific energy materials, such as lithium metal or NMC cathodes, our study employs Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) cathode material for several strategic reasons. He et al. [8] highlighted that although solid-state batteries offer material-level safety advantages, they are not immune to thermal runaway at the cell level under extreme conditions. This underscores the importance of stable electrode-electrolyte interfaces in ensuring safe operation. LFP's exceptional structural stability and minimal volume changes during cycling are advantageous for maintaining stable interfaces with solid electrolytes, reducing the risk of interface degradation and thermal runaway [9]. Additionally, LFP's inherent thermal stability complements the safety advantages of solid-state electrolytes. Recent thermal safety assessments emphasize that interface stability and thermal management are critical in preventing thermal runaway cascades in solid-state cells [8,10]. This makes LFP an optimal choice for evaluating solid-state battery safety and thermal behavior.

The majority of battery researchers have predominantly focused on conventional LIBs, leading to the development of comprehensive mathematical models aimed at their analysis and the advancement of control strategies [11–14]. In contrast, the exploration of SSBs and their numerical modeling has been comparatively limited, often restricted to studies on individual components such as electrodes or small-scale coin cells. Current methodologies for macro-scale modeling this technology are divided into electrochemical-thermal coupled [15,16] and electrical models [17,18]. Although the first approach can accurately estimate the behavior and the three-dimensional temperature distribution of the battery, it requires a series of internal battery parameters and the cost of computational time is high [11,12]. On the other hand, electrical models leverage an Equivalent Circuit Model (ECMs) approach, based on a network of resistors and capacitors (RC branches), which effectively simulate the battery's voltage dynamics, with a lower computational time, suitable for different vehicle applications [19]. The importance of ECMs has been emphasized in Ref. [20], which explores how ECMs effectively capture battery behavior while considering long-term degradation and economic factors for grid applications. However, other studies indicated that incorporating Constant Phase Elements (CPEs) instead of traditional capacitors can enhance the accuracy of ECMs [21,22]. CPEs are widely used to account for the frequency-dependent behavior and distributed properties of the battery, offering improved representation of diffusion and interfacial effects in batteries [23,24]. Regarding the ECM available for SSBs, Celen et al. [18] presents a review of the ECMs configurations applied for different solid-states cells electrodes and electrolytes chemistries, where they found that most of the models available in literature contain two RC branches and proposed a model in a similar fashion. For the parameter's calibration, the authors used the DC internal resistance (DC-IR) information of the cell from the manufacturer as boundary conditions and considered the effect of two ambient temperatures. Hybrid Power Pulse Characterization (HPPC) test data was used for the model validation showing a mean absolute error of voltage below 30 mV. The cell materials and geometry that they modeled were not revealed due to confidentiality. Another dedicated review of the ECMs can be found in the work of Clearly et al. [25] but in specific for modeling the Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) SSB. The authors developed an ECM, not considering temperature effects, for a laboratory-self-made Li-S coin cell, with lithium-indium as anode material. Their model consists of a parameterized three-state ECM using low rate constant C-rate cycles and pulse power characterization data as reference. They proposed a nested combination of linear regression with non-linear particle swarm optimization for the estimation of the model parameters, capturing the slow cycling and fast pulse charge/discharge dynamics within 34.2 mV and 11.5 mV RMS errors, respectively. They concluded that the accurate modeling of the dependence of resistance on C-rate and state of charge (SOC) is important for developing a robust model.

However, only a few studies are related to the modeling of SSBs, especially the electrical part, but no one proposes an electro-thermal model for full SSBs. The thermal model Li-ion batteries, providing an efficient way to predict the temperature evolution during battery operation, has attracted increasing attention through experiments and modeling [26]. The importance of coupling electrical and thermal models to predict battery performance under various operating conditions is highlighted in Ref. [27], demonstrating improved accuracy through state-of-charge normalization approaches. There are two heat generation mechanisms in SSBs, reversible heat generation due to entropy change and irreversible heat generation due to electrochemical polarization [28]. For temperature estimation in the battery, existing partial differential equation-based thermal models can predict the detailed temperature distribution throughout the battery [29–31]. However, these models are more computationally intensive. Therefore, single-state models, which represent thermal dynamics using a bulk temperature, are widely used for their computational efficiency [28,30]. Additionally, two-state thermal models that capture surface and core temperatures have been studied [32–34]. These models can estimate core temperature and are efficient for control applications due to their limited number of states. To our knowledge, various modelling approaches exist for conventional lithium-ion batteries. However, comprehensive electro-thermal models applied for solid-state batteries remain scarce in the literature. Most studies focus either on individual components or small-scale cells, leaving a significant gap in literature related to the modelling applications of full-scale solid-state batteries. Our work addresses this gap by providing a complete electro-thermal characterization and modeling framework for high-capacity solid-state batteries. Moreover, models that balance accuracy with computational efficiency are essential for practical applications in embedded devices with limited memory capacity, including but not limited to EVs.

Motivated by all the above, this paper aims to develop a comprehensive electro-thermal model for high-capacity 30 Ah SSB pouch cells, integrating both electrical and thermal aspects unique to SSBs. The main contributions of this work are.
•
Experimental Characterization Methodology: A detailed methodology is proposed for the experimental characterization of SSBs, including capacity tests, open-circuit voltage measurements, hybrid pulse power characterization, and thermal evaluations, to identify the dependencies of key parameters on state of charge and temperature.
•
Electro-Thermal Model Development: An electro-thermal model is proposed that combines a third-order equivalent circuit model to capture the complex impedance behavior unique to SSBs and a lumped thermal model to predict temperature evolution. The model incorporates both irreversible heat from internal resistance and reversible heat associated with entropy changes, determined through a validated method correlating open-circuit voltage variations with temperature.
•
Two-Way Coupling and Optimization: A two-way coupling mechanism between the electrical and thermal models is implemented, where the electrical model feeds the thermal model with generated heat, and the thermal model provides updated temperatures to adjust electrical parameters accordingly. Both fitting and optimization algorithms are compared to determine the model parameters, enhancing prediction accuracy.
•
Model Validation and Performance Evaluation: The proposed model is validated against experimental data from high-capacity SSB pouch cells, demonstrating high accuracy in predicting electrical performance and surface temperature under various operating conditions. Comparative analyses show that the proposed model outperforms existing ones in both accuracy and computational efficiency.

The structure of the paper is methodically organized as follows: Section 2 covers the experimental setup and testing procedures and details the methodology development process of the electro-thermal model for SSB cells. Section 3 presents the results obtained, comparing them with test data and discussing the model's accuracy, while also outlining potential directions for future research. Finally, Section 4 offers a comprehensive summary of the key findings and insights.

This section details the experimental setup and procedures used to characterize the 30 Ah solid-state battery and to obtain data for model parameterization and validation. The cell studied is a second-generation pouch cell with a nominal capacity of 30 Ah developed by Avesta Battery & Energy Engineering (ABEE) [35]. The main specifications are listed in Table 1 . The specified charge and discharge cut-off voltages for this cell are 3.65 V and 2 V, respectively. This battery cell is constructed with a Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) cathode, Silicon Carbide (SiC) anode, and a solid polymer electrolyte.

The experimental setup for the battery is shown in Fig. 2 it consists of the following components.
1.
Battery cycler: A Chroma battery tester capable of up to 5 V and 300 A, used for precise control of charging and discharging protocols.
2.
Thermal chamber: An LP/GDW-225 climate chamber (Hardy Technology, China) with a temperature range of −40 °C–150 °C, providing a controlled thermal environment.
3.
Data acquisition system: Connected via a Controller Area Network (CAN) communication line to record voltage, current, and temperature data.
4.
Temperature measurement: A single thermocouple attached to the center of the cell's surface to monitor temperature changes.
This experimental platform is subsequently used for battery capacity, open circuit voltage (OCV), hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC), and thermal tests.

The Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) versus SOC test is a critical component of the cell characterization protocol. This test was conducted at three ambient temperatures (15 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C) to determine the dependency of the OCV on SOC and temperature. There is a variety of procedures for this test [36] in literature. The one followed in this work consists of the following steps.
1.
Initial Charging: The cell is fully charged at a rate of 3 A (0.1C) using the CC-CV protocol, followed by a 2-h rest period to stabilize the battery.
2.
Discharge Pulses: A series of discharge pulses, each reducing 5 % of the cell's capacity, is applied. Each pulse is followed by a 2-h rest interval, repeated until the cell is fully discharged.

Similarly, the battery recharging process is conducted by interspersing 5 % recharge cycles with 2-h rest intervals, continuing this sequence until the cell achieves full charge. The data points chosen for plotting the open circuit voltage curve correspond to the measurements taken after each 2-h rest period. This selection criterion is based on the premise that the battery attains an equilibrium state during these intervals.

The HPPC is performed to assess the polarization and relaxation characteristics of the SSBs, which is commonly used to identify the ECM model parameters. A test defined in the Idaho National Laboratory [37], involves a specific protocol, which is illustrated in Fig. 7a, encompassing the following stages.
1.
Initial charging: The cell is charged at 7.5 A according to the CC-CV protocol
2.
Rest period: The cell rests for 1 h.
3.
Discharge pulse: A 7.5 A (0.5C) discharge pulse is applied for 10 s, followed by a 60-s rest period. This is followed by a 10-s charge pulse at 7.5 A (0.5C) and another 60-s rest period. The cell is then continuously discharged at 7.5 A (0.5C) to reduce the SOC by 5 %.
4.
Repetition: The last two steps are repeated until the cell is fully discharged.
The HPPC tests are conducted at three different ambient temperatures (15 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C), with voltage and current data recorded during each pulse.

The thermal test characterizes the cell's thermal response under varying current conditions. In this test, the battery is subjected to symmetrical, periodic current pulses of ±60 A (2C) for 2-s intervals, with 1-s rest periods between each charge and discharge pulse. This ensures that the SOC remains constant at 50 % throughout the experiment. The battery is placed in a thermal chamber with forced air cooling, and a K-type thermocouple attached to the battery surface to monitor its temperature. The experiment continues until thermal stability is achieved.

This section describes the development process of the electro-thermal model for the SSB. Fig. 3 illustrates the overall modeling workflow, highlighting the integration of experimental tests, model development, and validation. The model is composed of two coupled sub-models implemented in MATLAB/Simulink: an equivalent circuit model to capture the electrical behavior, and a lumped thermal model to simulate the thermal behavior of the cell. A two-way coupling mechanism is implemented, where the electrical model provides heat generation data to the thermal model, and the thermal model supplies temperature data to update the electrical parameters accordingly.

In this study, the ECM with RC (Resistance and Capacitance) is adopted to describe the dynamic characteristics of the SSB. Fig. 4 illustrates the scheme of an n-RC ECM, which comprises an OCV denoted by
V
O C V

R 0

networks. Each network includes a polarization resistor
R i

C i

network is denoted as
V i

V
t e r m i n a l
signifies the terminal voltage. The applied current
I
is considered negative during discharge and positive during charging.

A critical aspect of the modeling procedure involves selecting the proper configuration, particularly the number of RC networks, to accurately reproduce the cell's transient behavior [38,39]. Therefore, to determine the optimum number of RC pairs, an analysis was performed focusing on the relaxation event recorded during HPPC tests. This analysis involved fitting the relaxation voltage curve ranging from one to four RC pairs, as represented by Eq. (6). Fig. 5 shows that the three-RC network model provides a significantly accurate fit to the experimental data compared to models with one or two RC pairs. While a four-RC network would offer a marginal improvement in accuracy, this enhancement is marginal and comes at the cost of increased model complexity. Specifically, a four-RC network increases the computational demand without a substantial enhancement in fitting precision, mainly when evaluated against the minimal difference between the three and four-RC configurations. Furthermore, using a three-RC model allows a more efficient parameterization process while maintaining a high level of accuracy. Consequently, the three-RC network model was adopted for this study to ensure an optimal trade-off between model complexity and computational efficiency.

In this equation, the OCV, resistors, and capacitors' values are functions of the SOC and temperature. To accurately identify these parameters, a procedure based on the data obtained from the HPPC tests can be used [42–44].

The ECM was developed using the Simscape library from Simulink [45]. In this way, it was built based on blocks that represent OCV, resistors, and capacitors. To reflect the dependence of the cell's behavior on SOC and temperature, different values for all the blocks were used based on these parameters. These values are organized into a Look-Up Table (LUT), constructed from experimental data, which allows the model to accurately replicate the behavior of the cell under varying operating conditions.

To determine of the reversible heat term, the common approach in the literature involves placing the cell at a certain SOC in a climate chamber and varying the temperature [32,51–53]. The open circuit voltage

However, this conventional method of determining the reversible heat

is complex and time consuming. Therefore, in this work the methodology proposed by Geifes et al. [54] was adopted, which involves extracting the entropic coefficient from the pulse relaxation measurements of the OCV test. For this purpose, the OCV was recorded at three different temperatures as described in Section 2.2. For each defined SOC the voltage changes were analyzed with respect to the temperature variations.

Fig. 6 a shows the open circuit voltage as function of temperature for an SOC of 95 %, while Fig. 6b shows the OCV over the temperature for an SOC of 30 %. A linear fit through the depicted voltage values (T) is calculated using Eq (14):

In this study, two different methods for electrical parameters identification have been evaluated: the Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS) optimization and the Curve Fitting (CF) method.

The NLS approach is a widely used technique for battery parameter identification. This method employs iterative algorithms such as Gauss-Newton, Levenberg-Marquardt, Trust-Region Reflective (TRR), Conjugate Gradient, BFGS and Nelder-Mead Simplex [55]. These algorithms start with initial estimations of the model parameters and iteratively refine them to minimize the sum of squared residuals the model predictions and the experimental data. In this study, the Trust-Region Reflective-based NLS method was chosen. This method is particularly efficient in handling a large number of parameters, providing a balance and exploiting the solution space. This balance helps the algorithm converge to the global minimum, reducing the risk of being trapped in a local minimum [56].

data point.

The CF approach involves fitting exponential functions to the battery's terminal voltage curve obtained from HPPC tests. This method is widely used for parameter identification in battery modeling [57–60]. Specifically, the voltage curve is segmented into distinct time intervals, where each segment corresponds to phases of rapid and gradual relaxation. Within these intervals, the model parameters such as the ohmic resistance
R 0
and the polarization resistances and capacitances (
R i
, and
C i
) are extracted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameter identification for the thermal model
ce:section.ce:para:
Accurate thermal parameter identification is essential for predicting the temperature evolution of the battery during operation. The parameters identified for the thermal model are the specific heat capacity
C p
and the convective heat transfer coefficient
h
.

The objective function of the thermal model is defined as Eq (15):
(15)
J
( θ )
=
1 2

(

)

)
]
2
Where,
T
s , exp
is the experimentally measured cell surface temperature at
n
data point, and
T
s , s i m
is the cell surface temperature obtained from the model simulation at n data point.

The accuracy of the proposed model has been assessed by evaluating the key goodness of fit indicators. The precision of the instantaneous response has been mainly evaluated by the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to quantify the discrepancies between the experimental measurements and the model simulations for voltage and temperature. Additionally, the probability density function (PDF) of the errors was analyzed to evaluate the distribution and magnitude of the deviations.
(16)

Where n denotes the number of data points within the identification interval,
V a r i a b l e

represents the measured variable (voltage or temperature) at step i-th, while
V a r i a b l e
s i m
is the corresponding simulation variable resulting from the model at i-th step.

This section presents the experimental data gathered for the SSB samples, the parameter identification for the equivalent circuit model, and the validation of the electro-thermal model against experimental data. The accuracy and predictive capability of the model are discussed in detail.

Fig. 7a and b shows the charge test protocol previously detailed in the experimental methodology. In contrast, Fig. 7c and d displays the discharge test protocol. The OCV evolution with respect to SOC is calculated as the average voltage of two corresponding profiles of discharge and charge cycles with 0.1C-rate at the same SOC. Fig. 8 illustrates the OCV-SOC relation results obtained for the three different ambient temperatures considered in the experimental characterization. It can be observed that, except for a condition close to cell full discharge (SOC = 0), the OCV hardly changes with temperature. In fact, when the battery SOC is in the 15 %–95 % range, the effect is almost negligible. Thus, these results indicate that the OCV depends significantly more on SOC than on temperature.

The HPPC characterization process spanned approximately one day, accounting for resting intervals between pulses at a single ambient temperature. The tests were conducted across three distinct temperatures: 15 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C. During each pulse, voltage and current measurements were recorded. Fig. 9 a and b presents the HPPC current and voltage responses from one of the experiments conducted at 25 °C respectively. For thermal characterization, a separate test protocol was implemented as described in section 2.1.3. Fig. 10 illustrates the experimental cell surface temperature obtained during this thermal test at a constant ambient temperature of 25 °C.

Two methods were employed to determine the parameters (R0, Ri, and Ci) of the ECM: the Curve Fitting method and the Trust-Region Reflective Nonlinear Least Squares algorithm, as detailed in Section 2.2.3. The estimated model parameters at 25 °C, obtained from curve fitting and TRR are shown in Fig. 11 . The first thing that can be observed from the comparison is that the internal resistance parameter (
R 0
)
differs when comparing both methodologies. Additionally, there is a divergence between the two methods around the state of charge of 0.1 and 0.95. The TRR-NLS method exhibits a slight deviation from the curve fitting line, which appears relatively constant across the SOC range.

The polarization parameters estimated by the CF and TRR methods exhibit notable differences across the SOC range. Both approaches yield relatively consistent values regarding the ohmic resistance (R1), with the TRR method generally predicting slightly higher values. This suggests that the SOC does not significantly influence the ohmic resistance and that the TRR method may capture this parameter more accurately. Interestingly, a trade-off between the polarization resistances (R2 and R3) and capacitances (C2 and C3) is observed. At certain SOC regions, one polarization resistance exhibits a higher value while the corresponding capacitance is lower, and vice versa. Both methods polarization parameters remain relatively constant within the SOC range (10–90 %). However, at low and high SOC levels, nonlinearities dominate the OCV-SOC relationship, leading to significant deviations in the polarization parameters. In terms of performance of the model, the curve fitting approach provides a root mean square error when predicting the behaviour of the cell during a HPPC test at 25 °C of
R M S E
v
= 0.45
V
. In contrast, for the same data, the TRR algorithm yields a significantly lower
R M S E
v
= 0.0163
V
, indicating an excellent fit between the experimental data and the simulation. This demonstrates that the TRR algorithm is more accurate than the curve fitting approach.

The electrical parameters of the ECM, including ohmic and polarization resistances as well as polarization capacitance were extracted as functions of temperature and SOC at different temperatures. Fig. 11 illustrates the optimal electric parameters obtained through TRR at 15 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C. Fig. 12 R0 shows that internal resistance exhibits sensitivity to temperature variations while remaining notably stable across the 10–90 % SOC range, with values comparable to other solid-state batteries for Li-S solid-state cells [61] and for sulfide-based solid electrolytes [62] and significantly lower than those reported for liquid electrolyte batteries (typically 50–200 mΩ) [63,64]. Fig. 12b, c, and 12d demonstrate that the polarization resistance
R i
is sensitive to temperature and SOC. However, the parameter
C i
exhibits sensitivity to temperature while SOC seems to have a lesser impact. These results highlight the importance of considering temperature within the ECM for an accurate prediction of the cell performance and confirm the necessity of coupling it with a thermal model in order to simulate thermal behaviour. In this work, with the data obtained at different SOC and temperatures, a Look-Up Table (LUT) was created for each parameter and was included in the model.

Fig. 13 shows the calculated entropy coefficient over the state of charge range. The first thing that can be observed is that the entropy coefficient becomes negative for SOC values below approximately 30 % and positive for higher SOC levels. Between 50 % and 80 % SOC, the entropy coefficient stabilizes around 0.2 mV/K. Similar entropy coefficient profiles have been reported by Lenz et al. [65].

The thermal parameters that must be determined for the lumped thermal model are the specific heat capacity (Cp) and the heat transfer coefficient (h). These parameters can be identified by utilizing the thermal measurements tests conducted at 25 °C. Geifes et al. [49] calculated the heat transfer resistance from the thermal steady-state data, while the heat capacity is derived from the transient temperature evolution. Therefore, a parameter estimation approach based on the Trust-Region Reflective method is applied to extract the thermal parameters by adjusting the model output to fit the experimental measurements of the cell temperatures. The heat generation calculated from Eq. (12) is used as an input to the thermal model. Table 2 presents the optimal parameter values obtained. The specific heat capacity is found to be 1102.82
J / K . k g
, and the heat transfer coefficient is 12.11
W /
m 2
. K
.

Once the electrical and thermal parameters are calculated, the electro-thermal model can be used to predict the SSB behaviour. However, validation is required to identify its accuracy. In this work, the Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization tests have been used as reference. Thus, the model's accuracy has been evaluated by comparing the terminal voltage predicted by the model and actually measured under diverse ambient temperatures. Both experimental data and simulations are shown in Fig. 14 . Specifically, Fig. 14a, b, and c display the model's predictive outcomes at ambient temperatures of 15 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C, respectively. The deviation between the model predicted terminal voltages and the observed values across these varying temperature conditions is depicted in Fig. 14d. It can be noticed that the outcome of the model shows relatively good agreement with experimental data. The maximum modeling error is 0.7 %, and the average error is 0.23 % within the 5–95 % SOC range (similar to lithium-ion batteries, which primarily operate between 20 and 80 % SOC [66]). Noticeably, the error tends to increase as the battery cell discharges, resulting in higher prediction errors at low SOC conditions.

Furthermore, Fig. 15 shows the error distribution's probability density function (PDF), indicating that the variances in voltage predictions remain within acceptable limits
[
− 0.05 V , + 0.05 V
]
across the examined temperature ranges. The PDF does not show noticeable peaks for errors larger than 0.05 V due to the very small number of data points with such errors, particularly in the low SOC range.

To complete the validation, Table 3 summarizes the fit indicators corresponding to the different temperatures within the HPPC profile. It can be observed that as the temperature increases, the RMSE decreases. For instance, varies from 0.0165V at 15 °C to 0.0091V at 45 °C.

The thermal test measurements at 25 °C and 45 °C ambient temperatures are used for validation. The temperature simulation results, and corresponding experimental data are compared, as shown in Fig. 16 . Fig. 16a shows the comparison at
T a
= 25
° C
, showing that both the simulated and experimental temperature profiles rise rapidly at the beginning and stabilize around 27 °C. The simulated data (red line) closely follows the experimental data (blue line), demonstrating the model's accuracy. Fig. 16b depicts the comparison at
T a
= 45
° C
. Similar to the 25 °C case, the simulated and experimental temperature profiles show a rapid rise at the start, stabilizing around 46.5 °C. While the simulated data aligns well with the experimental data, minor fluctuations in the experimental data can be observed, which may be attributed to measurement noise.

Furthermore, the probability density function of the temperature discrepancies shown in Fig. 17 demonstrates that the discrepancies in temperature prediction are contained within the interval [−0.25 °C, +0.25 °C] across the examined temperature ranges. The concentration of peaks near zero discrepancy for both temperatures highlights the model's overall accuracy in predicting the temperatures.

The proposed modeling strategy can accurately predict the terminal voltage and temperature response of SSBs at different temperatures with higher estimation accuracy. While this study presents a comprehensive electro-thermal model for solid-state batteries, several avenues remain open for further exploration and enhancement. The current lumped thermal model assumes uniform temperature distribution across the cell. Future work could incorporate spatially resolved thermal models, enabling a more detailed analysis of temperature gradients within the battery. Despite the effectiveness of the proposed electro-thermal model, a notable limitation is the reliance on adjusted electrochemical and thermal parameters. These parameters, while necessary to achieve accurate predictions, reduce the generalization and predictive capability of the model for different configurations. To address this in future work, advanced parameter estimation methods that minimize reliance on empirical adjustments could be explored, such as machine learning algorithms approach. Additionally, experimental studies could focus on directly measuring key thermal properties, such as specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity, to reduce dependency on adjusted thermal parameters. These enhancements would further improve the robustness and applicability of the model in diverse scenarios.

This paper proposes an electro-thermal model for a 30 Ah next-generation all-solid-state battery. The model integrates an equivalent-circuit electrical model with a lumped thermal model, implementing bidirectional coupling through heat generation and temperature-dependent electrical parameters. The main conclusions of the research are listed below.
1.
The electro-thermal model demonstrates high accuracy in predicting both electrical and thermal behavior of SSBs cells. The voltage predictions maintain deviations within
[
− 0.05
V , + 0.05
V
]
, and with temperature predictions achieving accuracy within
[
− 0.25
° C , + 0.25
° C
]
. These deviations are more pronounced in the low SOC region.
2.
Implementation of the trust-region reflective-based nonlinear least squares algorithm significantly improved parameter estimation compared to traditional curve fitting approaches. The results prove that the TRR algorithm shows good accuracy with a
R M S E
V
= 0.0163
V
.
3.
The accuracy of the thermal model is improved by considering the entropic heat generation.

The methodology presented in this paper demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed modeling approach, which could be highly beneficial for further state monitoring and control in battery management systems.
